introSection: Flashback was a steel roller coaster which operated at Six Flags Magic Mountain in Valencia, California, USA. Before it came to Magic Mountain, it operated at two other Six Flags parks under the name of Z-Force. It was designed and built by Intamin and Giovanola.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
In 1984, Six Flags Great America announced that they would be adding Z-Force. It would be a prototype Space Diver purchased from the Intamin testing facility. The ride opened on July 10, 1985. It operated for three seasons before closing in 1987. Later that year, it was announced that Z-Force would be moved to Six Flags Over Georgia.

Z-Force would open in April 1988 at Six Flags Over Georgia. After the 1991 season, it was removed from the park. That year, it was announced that the coaster would be relocated to Six Flags Magic Mountain under its new name, Flashback for the 1992 season. Flashback opened on April 25, 1992.

Flashback last operated in 2003. It was then left standing but not operating.

On January 23, 2007, a spokeswoman for the park confirmed that both Flashback and Psyclone would be removed. However, while Psyclone was demolished the following month, Flashback remained standing until December. The coaster was later listed up for sale by Six Flags on International Rides Management in November 2007, but it was never sold. Originally, the park stated that Flashback may be rebuilt from scratch within the park for the 2008 season, but the coaster was scrapped instead.

In circa 2013, after Flashback's former spot had stood empty for many years, Six Flags Hurricane Harbor expanded into it. However, the entrance to Flashback still stands and is used for a maze during Fright Fest.

sections.title: Design
sections.content: The ride starts with the chain lift hill and subsequently features five hairpin dives and an upward helix. The hairpin dives are supposed to mock a Fighter Jet. Flashback was the only Space Diver to ever be produced.

sections.title: Trains
sections.content: The trains were built by Giovanola and were the first to have four-abreast seating, now used on many Bolliger & Mabillard coasters.

sections.title: Incidents
sections.content: On July 17, 1989 an 11-year-old boy died while riding Z-Force at Six Flags Over Georgia. An investigation found that the boy had an undiagnosed underlying condition which was aggravated by the ride, and that he died of a "seizure-like disorder". The roller coaster reopened on July 22.

fullText:
Flashback was a steel roller coaster which operated at Six Flags Magic Mountain in Valencia, California, USA. Before it came to Magic Mountain, it operated at two other Six Flags parks under the name of Z-Force. It was designed and built by Intamin and Giovanola.

In 1984, Six Flags Great America announced that they would be adding Z-Force. It would be a prototype Space Diver purchased from the Intamin testing facility. The ride opened on July 10, 1985. It operated for three seasons before closing in 1987. Later that year, it was announced that Z-Force would be moved to Six Flags Over Georgia.

Z-Force would open in April 1988 at Six Flags Over Georgia. After the 1991 season, it was removed from the park. That year, it was announced that the coaster would be relocated to Six Flags Magic Mountain under its new name, Flashback for the 1992 season. Flashback opened on April 25, 1992.

Flashback last operated in 2003. It was then left standing but not operating.

On January 23, 2007, a spokeswoman for the park confirmed that both Flashback and Psyclone would be removed. However, while Psyclone was demolished the following month, Flashback remained standing until December. The coaster was later listed up for sale by Six Flags on International Rides Management in November 2007, but it was never sold. Originally, the park stated that Flashback may be rebuilt from scratch within the park for the 2008 season, but the coaster was scrapped instead.

In circa 2013, after Flashback's former spot had stood empty for many years, Six Flags Hurricane Harbor expanded into it. However, the entrance to Flashback still stands and is used for a maze during Fright Fest.

The ride starts with the chain lift hill and subsequently features five hairpin dives and an upward helix. The hairpin dives are supposed to mock a Fighter Jet. Flashback was the only Space Diver to ever be produced.

Blue track and white supports.

The trains were built by Giovanola and were the first to have four-abreast seating, now used on many Bolliger & Mabillard coasters.

On July 17, 1989 an 11-year-old boy died while riding Z-Force at Six Flags Over Georgia. An investigation found that the boy had an undiagnosed underlying condition which was aggravated by the ride, and that he died of a "seizure-like disorder". The roller coaster reopened on July 22.
